food_subgroup: Mushrooms and fungi — blanched and chilled preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Matsutake mushroom sashimi-style preparation. Mapped to the closest parent class 'mushroom food product' (FOODON_03301765) as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mushrooms and other fungi — fresh or chilled
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Mushrooms and other fungi) used as the closest defensible category. No specific Matsutake or sashimi-style mushroom code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Matsutake mushroom sashimi preparation. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard appetizer serving of Matsutake Mushroom Sashimi is approximately 80 grams, consisting of thinly sliced blanched Matsutake mushrooms arranged on crushed ice, served with a small dish of light soy sauce dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Mushrooms, shiitake, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169403); supplemented with Matsutake mushroom compositional data from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for edible fungi
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Matsutake mushroom (Tricholoma matsutake) in sashimi-style preparation. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy shiitake mushroom (raw) and USDA edible fungi compositional data as the closest approved-source equivalent. Matsutake is compositionally similar to shiitake in macronutrients and micronutrients. Sodium value (280 mg/100g) reflects the contribution of the soy sauce dipping sauce component included in the dish as prepared. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with the input record and cross-referenced against USDA edible fungi data. Vitamin B12 (0.1 mcg/100g) is a trace amount reported for some edible fungi and is retained with low confidence. Vitamin D (3.5 mcg/100g) is plausible for sun-dried or UV-exposed mushrooms; retained as estimated. All values documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mushrooms and low-carbohydrate vegetables — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for edible fungi and non-starchy vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Matsutake mushroom sashimi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entries for edible mushrooms and non-starchy vegetables, which consistently fall in the GI range of 10–20. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 3.04) / 100 ≈ 0.5. Carbohydrate per 80g serving = 3.8 × 0.8 = 3.04g. This is consistent with the low-carbohydrate, high-fibre profile of Matsutake mushrooms.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce dipping sauce (280 mg/100g as prepared)
hypertension.guidance: The mushroom itself is naturally low in sodium and rich in potassium (380 mg/100g), which supports blood pressure regulation; however, patients with hypertension should use the soy sauce dipping sauce sparingly or substitute with a low-sodium alternative to keep total sodium intake within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.8g/100g) and high dietary fibre (2.5g/100g) resulting in a low glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of 0.5 per 80g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Matsutake Mushroom Sashimi is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic impact; the high beta-glucan fibre content may further support postprandial blood glucose control, and the dish can be included freely in a diabetes-friendly meal plan provided the soy sauce dipping sauce is used in moderation to limit added sodium and any residual sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low total fat (0.5g/100g), low saturated fat (0.07g/100g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium content supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is cardioprotective in its macronutrient profile — extremely low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free — and the potassium content (380 mg/100g) supports healthy vascular tone; individuals with cardiovascular disease should nonetheless monitor total sodium intake from the soy sauce component and choose a low-sodium dipping sauce where possible.

gout.key_factor: Mushrooms contain moderate levels of purines
gout.guidance: Individuals with gout or hyperuricaemia should consume Matsutake mushrooms in moderation, as mushrooms contain moderate purine levels that may contribute to elevated uric acid; portion sizes of 80g or less are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Matsutake Mushroom Sashimi (松茸刺身, Sōngróng cìshēn) is a luxurious and minimalist Chinese appetizer originating from the Yunnan and Sichuan provinces of Southwest China. It celebrates the Matsutake mushroom (Tricholoma matsutake), prized as the 'king of mushrooms' for its intense pine-like, floral, and woody aroma and its firm, meaty texture. In this preparation, fresh Matsutake mushrooms are meticulously cleaned, thinly sliced, and briefly blanched to ensure food safety while preserving their raw-like qualities and delicate fragrance. The slices are artfully arranged on a bed of crushed ice, which accentuates their crisp texture and allows the complex aroma to remain the primary sensory focus. The dish is traditionally served with a simple dipping sauce of light soy sauce, sometimes enhanced with a touch of wasabi or fresh lime juice. Nutritionally, Matsutake Mushroom Sashimi is exceptionally low in calories (28 kcal/100g) and carbohydrates (3.8g/100g), with a meaningful protein content (3.0g/100g) and dietary fibre (2.5g/100g) from beta-glucan polysaccharides. It provides notable amounts of niacin (4.5mg/100g), riboflavin (0.35mg/100g), potassium (380mg/100g), phosphorus (150mg/100g), and iron (1.5mg/100g). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The sodium content (280mg/100g as prepared with dipping sauce) warrants attention for those with hypertension. The dish is vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and naturally gluten-free in its mushroom component, though the soy sauce dipping sauce contains gluten and soy allergens. It is best served as a premium appetizer or luxury starter at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: Is Matsutake sashimi good for diabetics?
What does Matsutake mushroom taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Matsutake mushroom (Tricholoma matsutake) in a sashimi-style blanched preparation. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy shiitake mushroom (raw) and USDA edible fungi compositional data as the closest approved-source equivalent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for edible fungi and non-starchy vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because Matsutake is compositionally well-characterised in the scientific literature and the adapted values are internally consistent and plausible.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE ADAPTATION: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Matsutake mushroom sashimi. Values adapted from USDA SR Legacy shiitake mushroom (raw, FDC ID 169403) and USDA edible fungi compositional data. Sodium (280 mg/100g) reflects the soy sauce dipping sauce component as part of the dish as prepared. 2. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g (0.07g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.06g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.22g) are estimated from USDA edible fungi fatty acid profiles; these are not directly reported for Matsutake in an approved source and are documented as estimated. 3. WATER: water_g set to -1 as detailed moisture data for blanched and chilled Matsutake mushroom is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 4. VITAMIN B12: vitamin_b12_mcg (0.1 mcg/100g) is a trace amount reported for some edible fungi; retained with low confidence as some studies report trace B12 in Matsutake. 5. VITAMIN D: vitamin_d_mcg (3.5 mcg/100g) is plausible for UV-exposed or sun-dried Matsutake mushrooms; retained as estimated from USDA mushroom vitamin D data. 6. VITAMIN A: vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed as naturally zero for Matsutake mushrooms, which contain no significant beta-carotene or retinol. 7. CHOLESTEROL: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based fungal food product. 8. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero for a minimally processed mushroom dish with no hydrogenated fats. 9. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for edible fungi and non-starchy vegetables. No direct GI measurement for Matsutake mushroom sashimi exists in the approved GI database. 10. DUPLICATE PORTION: The input record contained two identical portion entries (both '1 serving (80g)', 80g); deduplicated to one default serving. 11. GOUT CAUTION: Individuals with gout should consume mushrooms in moderation due to moderate purine content. 12. ALIASES CONSISTENCY: All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
